title: Transport
shortSummary: Catch an auto-rickshaw, take the train, and get around Indian cities in Hindi.
culturalNote: Getting around India is an adventure with many options: the auto-rickshaw (ऑटो), the cycle-rickshaw, buses, the metro in big cities, and the famous Indian Railways. Auto drivers may quote a price rather than use the meter, so agreeing the fare before you sit down (किराया कितना है?) saves trouble. App-based cabs are now common in cities and remove the bargaining. Trains are a way of life, with classes from general seating to air-conditioned sleeper coaches, and a platform (प्लेटफ़ॉर्म) number is always worth confirming. The metro in Delhi and other cities is clean, cheap, and has a women's coach. A simple, friendly tone goes a long way; saying 'bhaiya, station chaloge?' (brother, will you go to the station?) to an auto driver is perfectly normal.

keyPhrases.native: क्या यह बस एयरपोर्ट जाती है?
keyPhrases.transliteration: kya yah bus airport jaati hai?
keyPhrases.english: Does this bus go to the airport?

keyPhrases.native: जल्दी चलिए, देर हो रही है
keyPhrases.transliteration: jaldi chaliye, der ho rahi hai
keyPhrases.english: Please go quickly, it's getting late
keyPhrases.pronunciation: JUL-dee CHUH-li-yay, dare hoh ruh-HEE hai

grammar.explanation: Chalna (चलना) means 'to go along/move'. The polite request is chaliye (please go), the informal is chalo. With a destination, the place comes first: station chaliye means 'please go to the station'.
grammar.commonMistake: Confusing chalna (to move/go along) with jaana (to go to); for telling a driver to set off, chaliye is natural.

grammar.explanation: Things that will happen take a future ending agreeing with the subject's gender and number. A feminine subject like train takes aayegi (will come) and jaayegi (will go); a masculine subject takes aayega and jaayega.
grammar.commonMistake: Using aayega for the feminine train; the correct form is aayegi.

dialogue.setup: Ben hails an auto-rickshaw and negotiates with the driver, Raju.

lines.speaker: Ben
lines.native: भैया, स्टेशन चलिए। किराया कितना है?
lines.transliteration: bhaiya, station chaliye. kiraaya kitna hai?
lines.english: Brother, go to the station. How much is the fare?

lines.speaker: Raju
lines.english: It will cost one hundred and fifty rupees.

lines.speaker: Ben
lines.native: थोड़ा ज़्यादा है। मीटर से चलिए।
lines.transliteration: thoda zyaada hai. meter se chaliye.
lines.english: That's a bit much. Please go by the meter.

lines.speaker: Ben
lines.native: जल्दी चलिए, ट्रेन कब आएगी पता नहीं।
lines.transliteration: jaldi chaliye, train kab aayegi pata nahin.
lines.english: Go quickly, I don't know when the train will come.

lines.speaker: Raju
lines.native: चिंता मत कीजिए, दस मिनट में पहुँच जाएँगे।
lines.transliteration: chinta mat kijiye, das minute mein pahunch jaayenge.
lines.english: Don't worry, we'll arrive in ten minutes.

quiz.question: How do you ask 'How much is the fare?' in Hindi?
quiz.explanation: Kiraaya means 'fare' and kitna hai asks 'how much'.

quiz.question: Which phrase asks an auto driver to use the meter?
quiz.explanation: Meter se chaliye means 'please go by the meter'.

quiz.question: ट्रेन कब _____? (When will the train come?)
quiz.explanation: Train is feminine, so the future form is aayegi.

quiz.question: What does 'यहाँ रोकिए (yahaan rokiye)' mean?
quiz.explanation: Yahaan means 'here' and rokiye is the polite 'please stop'.

quiz.question: Arrange: दीजिए / टिकट / एक (Please give one ticket)
quiz.explanation: Number, noun, then the polite verb dijiye at the end.
